coredata.dc:title: The effect of nanoparticles and humic acid on technology critical element concentrations in aqueous solutions with soil and sand
coredata.dc:creator: Stepka, Zane
Dror, Ishai
Berkowitz, Brian
coredata.dc:description: As a consequence of their growing use in electronic and industrial products, increasing amounts of technology critical elements (TCEs) are being released to the environment. Currently little is known about the fate of many of these elements. Initial research on their potential environmental impact identifies TCEs as emerging contaminants. TCE movement in the environment is often governed by water systems. Research on “natural” waters so far demonstrates that TCEs tend to be associated with suspended particulate matter (SPM), which influences TCE aqueous concentrations (here: concentration of TCEs in dissolved form and attached to SPM) and transport. However, the relative potential of different types of SPM to interact with TCEs is unknown. Here we examine the potential of various types of particulate matter, namely different nanoparticles (NPs; Al2O3, SiO2, CeO2, ZnO, montmorillonite, Ag, Au and carbon dots) and humic acid (HA), to impact TCE aqueous concentrations in aqueous solutions with soil and sand, and thus influence TCE transport in soil-water environments. We show that a combination of NPs and HA, and not NPs or HA individually, increases the aqueous concentrations of TCEs in soil solutions, for all tested NPs regardless of their type. TCEs retained on SPM, however, settle with time. In solutions with sand, HA alone is as influential as NPs+HA in keeping TCEs in the aqueous phase. Among NPs, Ag-NPs and Au-NPs demonstrate the highest potential for TCE transport. These results suggest that in natural soil-water environments, once TCEs are retained by soil, their partitioning to the aqueous phase by through-flowing water is unlikely. However, if TCEs are introduced to soil-water environments as part of solutions rich in NPs and HA, it is likely that NP and HA combinations can increase TCE stability in the aqueous phase and prevent their retention on soil and sand, thus facilitating TCE transport.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Partitioning of TCEs
Nano-particles
Retention
REEs
Platinum group elements

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: TCE retention by soil and sand without presence of NPs/HA
Effect of NPs and HA on aqueous concentrations of TCEs in soil solutions
Effect of NPs and HA on aqueous concentrations of TCEs in sand solutions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of National Infrastructure, Energy and Water Resources
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of National Infrastructure, Energy and Water Resources

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by a grant from the Ministry of National Infrastructure, Energy and Water Resources (Grant No. 215-17-016 ) in cooperation with the H2020 program “Water Joint Programming Initiative” through “WaterWorks2014” ERA-NET. The authors thank the Cathles group at Cornell University for providing samples of C-dots. B.B. holds the Sam Zuckerberg Professorial Chair in Hydrology. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Retention of technology critical elements by soil and sand: white background – solution A, grey background – solution B. Note: in solution B, Sn, Sb and Mo are not TCEs. Black squares: TCE concentration in solution without soil or sand; red circles: TCE concentration in the aqueous phase (here: concentrations of TCEs in dissolved form and attached to SPM) in the presence of sand; blue triangles: TCE concentration in e aqueous phase in presence of soil.
REE (solution A) concentrations in the aqueous phase of soil solutions in samples with each NP alone, HA alone, and each NP together with HA after 4 and 24h sample settling.
TCE concentrations in the aqueous phase of soil solution in the presence of NP alone, HA alone, and NP+HA. White background – solution A, grey background – solution B.
TCE concentrations in the aqueous phase of sand solution in presence of NP alone, HA alone, and NP+HA according to the NP. White background – solution A, grey background – solution B.
TCEs+NP+HA with 10, 30 and 50mgL−1 HA (note different line widths marking different HA concentrations) with soil, sand and no soil/sand. White background – solution A, grey background – solution B.
Aqueous concentrations of TCE as affected by different NPs a .

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: TCE concentration in the aqueous phase of solution is shown for experiments with NPs only and NP+HA for soil and sand solution. For TCE solution A (REEs), the average aqueous concentration of REEs is provided, as the different REEs show similar values. Size and zeta potential for C dots from Subramanian et al. (2013).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: pH of zeta potential measurements: ZnO-6.9; Ag – 6.9; Al2O3 – 7.1; SiO2 – 6.9; CeO2 – 6.7; Au – 6.8; Mont. – 7.0.

head.ce:title: The effect of nanoparticles and humic acid on technology critical element concentrations in aqueous solutions with soil and sand

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Earth and Planetary Sciences, Weizmann Institute of Science, Rehovot 7610001, Israel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Earth and Planetary Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a consequence of their growing use in electronic and industrial products, increasing amounts of technology critical elements (TCEs) are being released to the environment. Currently little is known about the fate of many of these elements. Initial research on their potential environmental impact identifies TCEs as emerging contaminants. TCE movement in the environment is often governed by water systems. Research on “natural” waters so far demonstrates that TCEs tend to be associated with suspended particulate matter (SPM), which influences TCE aqueous concentrations (here: concentration of TCEs in dissolved form and attached to SPM) and transport. However, the relative potential of different types of SPM to interact with TCEs is unknown. Here we examine the potential of various types of particulate matter, namely different nanoparticles (NPs; Al2O3, SiO2, CeO2, ZnO, montmorillonite, Ag, Au and carbon dots) and humic acid (HA), to impact TCE aqueous concentrations in aqueous solutions with soil and sand, and thus influence TCE transport in soil-water environments. We show that a combination of NPs and HA, and not NPs or HA individually, increases the aqueous concentrations of TCEs in soil solutions, for all tested NPs regardless of their type. TCEs retained on SPM, however, settle with time. In solutions with sand, HA alone is as influential as NPs+HA in keeping TCEs in the aqueous phase. Among NPs, Ag-NPs and Au-NPs demonstrate the highest potential for TCE transport. These results suggest that in natural soil-water environments, once TCEs are retained by soil, their partitioning to the aqueous phase by through-flowing water is unlikely. However, if TCEs are introduced to soil-water environments as part of solutions rich in NPs and HA, it is likely that NP and HA combinations can increase TCE stability in the aqueous phase and prevent their retention on soil and sand, thus facilitating TCE transport.

ce:section.ce:para: As a consequence of their growing use in electronic and industrial products, increasing amounts of technology critical elements (TCEs) are being released to the environment. TCEs include Nb, Ta, Ga, In, Ge, Tl, Te, the Platinum Group Elements (PGEs: Pt, Os, Ru, Rh, Pd and Ir), and most of the Rare Earth Elements (REEs: Y, La, Ce, Pr, Nd, Sm, Eu, Gd, Tb, Dy, Yb, Lu).

TCE applications in technologies range from light bulbs, to computer tablets, to general household appliances, to cars and military defense systems, and are integrated into every part of modern life (EU report, 2014). REE applications include: glass additives for optical lenses and display screens (Ce, La, Nd), pigments (La, Ce), petroleum catalysts and fluid cracking (mixed REE products), high intensity magnets for hard disk drives and hybrid vehicles (Nd, Dy), electric motors and audio equipment (Nd, Sm, Dy, Pr, Tb), batteries and hydrogen storage systems (mischmetal (alloy of REEs), lanthanum alloys), phosphors for display screens, fluorescent and halogen lamps, X-ray films (Y, Er, Tb, La, Gd, Ce), advanced ceramics (Y), capacitors (La, Nd, Ce), magnetic refrigeration (Gd), steel and foundry materials for desulphurization (mischmetal). PGEs are used mainly in catalytic converters in the car industry, but they also serve as excellent catalysts for many manufacturing processes, such as in the commercial production of nitric acid for nitrogen fertilizers (Tarentino and Maley, 1975; Eliseeva and Bünzli, 2011; Goonan, 2011; Jones, 2011; Wall, 2013).

Due to increased use in a variety of technological applications and resultant increased disposal to the environment, TCEs are undergoing a significant change in their cycle at the Earth's surface (Cobelo-García et al., 2015). Currently little is known about the fate and potential environmental impact for many of the TCEs. Initial work has detected increased amounts of anthropogenic Gd (Kulaksız and Bau, 2011a), La and Sm (Kulaksız and Bau, 2011b; Kulaksız and Bau, 2013; Merschel and Bau, 2015) in rivers and demonstrated human-released TCE transport into biota (Merschel and Bau, 2015). In a review of the results on ecotoxicological effects of REEs, Kulaksız and Bau (2013) argue that REEs have become emerging contaminants.

The one certainty about TCEs is that their release will increase: the global demand for REEs, for example, is expected to grow by hundreds of percent in the next 20years (Alonso et al., 2012). It is also clear that these elements will be released to the environment not as pure materials but rather as a mixture with many other substances. To evaluate possible environmental hazards and monitor TCEs, an understanding of TCE behavior in natural environments is required. In this study we address TCE aqueous concentrations in soil-water environments which are directly related to TCE mobility.

Current reported TCE concentrations present in natural environments vary in the range of 1 to 100ppt in river water (Gaillardet et al., 2014). However, in industrial effluent plumes, total REEs, for example, can be as high as >50,000ppb, with high concentrations of the light elements (Kulaksız and Bau, 2011a). Noack et al. (2014) reported REE concentrations in a wide range of water types; highest concentrations in groundwater were in the range of hundreds of ppb, in river water one order of magnitude lower, and a further order of magnitude lower in lake water, but the median values for all cases were much lower and usually in the range of 1.5 to 15ppt.

In natural environments, TCEs are transported either as “truly dissolved” species or via retention on suspended particulate matter (SPM) (this term is used hereafter to describe any nanoparticles, organic matter and colloids that aqueous phase TCEs can attach to). Currently available examples from groundwater, river, lake and marine surface waters and even from wastewater effluents show that, when mobile, TCEs are mostly associated with SPM (Andersson et al., 2006; Biddau et al., 2009; Pokrovsky et al., 2010; Shynu et al., 2011; Bau et al., 2013). Typical amounts of REEs, for example, associated with SPM vary from 30 to 90% of the dissolved REE pool in Connecticut River (Sholkovitz, 1992), to >95% in tropical rivers (Merschel et al., 2017; Gerard et al., 2003). In some cases (Elderfield et al., 1990; Sholkovitz, 1992, 1995; Kulaksız and Bau, 2013; Merschel and Bau, 2015), different behavior among REEs was reported, where light rare earth elements tend to bind to SPM while heavy rare earth elements are in the dissolved pool. From these examples, we hypothesize that TCE transport is mediated by SPM also in soil-water environments.

It is less clear, however, what specific particulate matter (PM) is suspended in soil-water environments and what SMP or SPM characteristics tend to be relevant for TCEs. Numerous studies on “natural” waters demonstrate that REEs are associated with different types of organic matter (e.g. Gerard et al., 2003; Merschel et al., 2017). Especially illustrative is work on estuaries which demonstrates that upon river water interaction with seawater, REEs are removed due to aggregation and sinking of SPM together with the REEs adsorbed on them (Boyle et al., 1977; Sholkovitz, 1993; Merschel et al., 2017), whereas REEs transported by organic rich rivers are much less susceptible to such estuarine removal (Pokrovsky and Schott, 2002; Merschel et al., 2017).

In parallel, nanoparticles (NPs; natural and engineered) have received considerable attention over the last two decades as a result of the fast growth in their production, use and consequent release to the environment (e.g. Nowack and Bucheli, 2007; Wigginton et al., 2007; Keller et al., 2010; Pu et al., 2016). Wigginton et al. (2007) indicated that because of their size and chemical properties, NPs have the ability to influence water chemistry processes in unique ways. The authors demonstrated these “NP-related effects” for natural NPs. Keller et al. (2010) reported on the suspension stability and behavior of selected engineered NPs in the presence of humic substances for different aqueous media, including groundwater, river and seawater. Ben-Moshe et al. (2010) investigated transport of engineered NPs in artificial porous media. Recently Tou et al. (2017) examined association of NPs with selected metals, emanating from effluents of wastewater treatment plants. Significantly, though, little attention has been paid to cross-linking interactions between NPs and TCEs.

Humic acid has also been demonstrated to increase engineered NP mobility through sand and soil (e.g. Yecheskel et al., 2016). While the specific bonding to organic compounds is unknown, REEs for example seem to have a particular affinity to carboxy-phenolic and phenolic groups (Gangloff et al., 2014). There is also an indication of different functional group preferences among different REEs (Marsac et al., 2012; Gangloff et al., 2014).

Regarding inorganic SPM, modeling has shown that up to 60% of river-water REEs could be associated with mineral colloids (Deberdt et al., 2002). Empirically, REEs have been shown to be associated with “natural” volcanic nanoparticles (Tepe and Bau, 2014) and clays (Merschel et al., 2017).

Although many indications show that in natural environments, SPM increases REE concentrations in the aqueous phase and therefore increases REE mobility, little is known about these interactions. It is further noted that studying these interactions is technically difficult to conduct on “natural” samples. We therefore investigated PM and TCE interactions in aqueous soil and sand environments, using batch experiments on samples with controlled contents. In particular, we used industrial quartz sand as an initial approximation to quartz-rich environments (in the experiments referred to as “sand solutions”) and local soil (referred to as “soil solutions”).

The overall objective of this study was to compare the potential of different NPs, HA, and NPs together with HA to retain TCEs, thus increasing their concentrations in the aqueous phase (here: concentration of TCEs in dissolved form and attached to SPM), and potentially serving to facilitate TCE transport in soil-water environments. Specifically, we examined the stability of TCEs remaining in the aqueous phase in soil and sand solutions. We compared TCE–PM interactions by mixing TCEs with different natural (SiO2, Al2O3 ZnO and montmorillonite) and anthropogenic/engineered nanoparticles (Ag-NPs, Au-NPs, CeO2-NPs and C dots), with and without presence of HA. In particular, we investigated the potential effect of NP size and surface charge, as these parameters determine the stability of NP suspensions.

Two multielement, standard solutions of TCEs were used as a source of TCEs: solution A containing 10mgL−1 Ce, Dy, Er, Eu, Gd, Ho, La, Nd, Pr, Sm, Tb, Tm, Y, Yb, Lu, Sc and Th in 5% HNO3 (Inorganic Ventures), and solution B containing 10mgL−1 Ge, Pd, Ru+5mgL−1 Ir, Ti and 10mgL−1 non-TCEs Sn, Sb and Mo in 10% HCl, 1% HNO3 and trace HF (Agilent Technology). Results for Sc, Th and Ti were not acquired, as the analytical method was not suitable for their quantification; these elements are not discussed further.

NPs: Al2O3 (30–60nm, stabilized corundum dispersion in water), SiO2 (<50nm, dispersion in water – stabilized non-crystalline colloidal SiO2), CeO2 (<25nm, dispersion in water), ZnO (<100nm, dispersion in water) and montmorillonite all purchased from Aldrich, Ag, Au nanoparticles were synthesized as described in Yecheskel et al. (2016) and further described in the Supplementary material (SM). Carbon dots were received from the Cathles group (Cornell University) and their synthesis is described in Krysmann et al. (2012) and Subramanian et al. (2013); further information is given in the SM. Humic acid sodium salt was purchased from Aldrich. Industrial quartz sand 50/70 (212–300μm; Accusand Specialty Quartz Sand) and red Mediterranean soil (4% clay, 96% sand, sieved to <250μm grain size) from the Weizmann Institute of Science campus were all used without any further purification. Further information on soil properties is given in the Supplementary material.

Four types of samples were analyzed: (i) each TCE solution in presence of soil or sand; (ii) each TCE solution+soil/sand+each NP, (iii) each TCE solution+soil/sand+HA, (iv) each TCE solution+soil/sand+each NP+HA. In addition, standards were measured with each TCE solution on its own and soil/sand on its own. Each sample was prepared by pouring into a 15mL tube, accurately weighed ~1g soil or sand, followed by adding 4mL of double distilled water (DDW), then 1mL pre-diluted 100mgL−1 NP or 500/300/100mgL−1 HA solution, and finally 5mL pre-diluted 200μgL−1 TCE solution which had been buffered to pH7 (±0.05) with NaOH.

In samples where both NP and HA were present, the sequence of addition was: ~1g soil/sand, 3mL DDW, 1mL 100mgL−1 NP, 5mL 200μgL−1 TCE solution and 1mL 50/30/10mgL−1 HA. (Note: an experiment where the time after NP/HA addition and before TCE solution addition was varied between 1min and 3h showed that this time difference does not influence the results; another experiment on samples with HA showed that variability in the final sample pH between 7.7 and 8.0 does not influence the results.) Samples were mixed by shaking for at least 48h (based on preliminary testing). After removal from the shaker, samples were left to settle for 4 or 24h, depending on the experiment (see Section 3.2). Then samples were prepared for analysis by transferring ~4mL solute with syringe from the top of the 15mL tube to an ICP vial and adding 55μL 70% HNO3. Further information regarding the procedure is given in the SM. We emphasize that measurements of TCE concentrations in the aqueous phase (see Section 2.3) reflect the total amount of TCE present in dissolved (aqueous) form together with any amount of TCE retained on the suspended NPs.
Each mixture was measured in three separately prepared samples, except for samples with NP+HA, where each of the triplicates had different HA concentration (10, 30 or 50mgL−1).

Initial concentrations in all experiments were as follows: TCEs were 100μgL−1 (all TCE stock solutions were diluted in the samples by factor of 100 which yielded 100μgL−1 for the majority of the TCEs, except Ir which was normalized to 100μgL−1 to allow convenient comparison), NPs were 10mgL−1, HA was 10, 30 or 50mgL−1. Note that the concentrations used are significantly higher than the average reported TCE concentrations in natural environments; as mentioned above, these concentrations are within the reported values of contaminated plumes in natural water bodies.

TCE concentrations were measured by inductively coupled plasma - mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Agilent 7700). The average standard deviation between the three samples of the same mixture was 1μgL−1, the highest standard deviation was 5μgL−1 (unless otherwise noted below). Results were normalized to 100.0μgL−1 initial TCE concentration, and 1.00g initial soil/sand weight.
Surface charge (zeta potential) of NPs was measured for each NP separately in an aqueous solution using a Zetasizer Nano ZSP (Malvern).

ce:section.ce:section-title: TCE retention by soil and sand without presence of NPs/HA
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the effect of NPs/HA on the aqueous concentration of TCE, first we need to establish TCE behavior in soil and sand solutions on their own. Retention of TCEs in two mixtures (solutions A and B) in soil and sand solutions is shown in Fig. 1 . “Retention” in this case is mostly due to sorption of TCEs on soil/sand and/or precipitation out of solution. All the studied elements of solution A showed ~100% retention by soil and ~93% by sand (Fig. 1). No differences were observed for retention of individual elements. In solution B, retention is element-dependent with Pd being retained most strongly (92% by soil and 85% by sand), followed by Ru (92% by soil and 73% by sand) and Ir (50% by soil and 15% by sand). Among all elements tested only Ir showed retention of only ~50% by soil. Ge is 82% retained by soil; it is the only element that was not retained by sand (the same as non-TCEs Sb and Mo). Mo was not retained by sand or soil. The retention differences between elements in solution B can result from different chemical properties and/or competition among elements.
When neither soil nor sand is present, the recovery of TCEs is 100%, demonstrating that no TCEs are retained on the vessel walls (“TCE sol. only” in Fig. 1).

In this experiment, we established the settling time to be used for revealing interactions between PM and TCEs in the particular samples. Alongside, results on SPM+TCE interactions, settling over time and relative NP effectiveness were gained.

Settling times of 4h and 24h were tested; 4h settling time experiments reveal interactions between SPM and TCEs (TCEs are detected in the aqueous phase: “Soil+NP+HA 4h” in Fig. 2 ), whereas after allowing samples to settle for 24h, nearly all the SPM had settled (almost no TCEs are detected in the aqueous phase). To detect interactions between SMP and TCEs, 4h settling time was used for all further experiments. It is noted that due to the demonstrated settling with time, results from these batch experiments can be used to detect presence/absence of increased TCE levels in the aqueous phase, which may indicate relative effectiveness of different NPs to keep the TCEs in the aqueous phase. But, because the measured aqueous TCE concentrations are dependent on settling time, their absolute values cannot be used as a sole parameter to quantify the increase in aqueous phase concentrations.

Fig. 2 shows the effect of adding NPs and/or HA on the concentrations of solution A elements (REEs) in the aqueous phase of soil solution. Recall that, as noted in Section 2.2, the measurements of TCE concentrations reflect the total amount of TCE present in dissolved (aqueous) form together with any TCEs retained on suspended NPs. Neither addition of any of the NPs alone, nor HA alone, significantly increased the concentrations of solution A elements in the aqueous phase (<5μgL−1 out of 100μgL−1) compared to solution A mixture with soil solution on its own. However, in samples where both NPs and HA were added, ~10–30μgL−1 of all solution A elements were measured in the aqueous phase after 4h settling. Such results suggest either that NP+REE and HA+REE suspensions settle faster than NP+HA+REE suspensions, or that in NP+REE and HA+REE experiments, REEs are removed directly from the aqueous solution by soil, whereas they are not removed directly by soil when both NP and HA are present (NP+HA+REE). A possible interpretation of such behavior is that humic acid creates a stabilizing “coating” on the NPs, which results in NPs with different surface properties than those of HA alone or NP alone, thus increasing the suspension stability and/or decreasing REE retention. The stabilization effect of HA on suspended NPs has been reported many times previously (e.g. Ben-Moshe et al., 2010; Keller et al., 2010). It is also possible that competition for retention by soil plays a role here: if HA fills potential sorption sites for NP-REEs on soil, or intervenes with REE precipitation out of solution, then the presence of HA will decrease TCE retention on soil.

After 24h settling, however, the increased REE concentration “signal” in the NP+HA+REE experiment aqueous phase is lost: REE concentrations in the aqueous phase are below 5μgL−1 for all NPs+HA except Ag and CeO2. Thus, (i) NP+HA+REE suspensions settle over time, and (ii) for Ag and CeO2 NPs, this settling is slower relative to the other NPs used, meaning that Ag and CeO2 are more effective in retaining REEs in the aqueous phase. For further discussion on significance of NP species, see Section 3.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of NPs and HA on aqueous concentrations of TCEs in soil solutions
ce:section.ce:para: A settling time of 4h was selected following the results of Section 3.2; experiments were conducted adding NPs, HA and NPs+HA to TCE solutions in the presence of soil and sand to establish the effect of these mixtures on TCE concentrations in the aqueous phase.

In solution B experiments with soil, similar to the observation made for solutions A earlier (Section 3.2), experiments involving NPs together with HA demonstrate elevated TCE concentrations in the solution compared to experiments with TCEs and soil solutions without any added PM (see labels “Soil” in Fig. 3 and Section 3.1) or with added NPs only or HA only; see Section 3.2 for interpretation of these results.

Experiments with NPs on their own or HA on its own did not increase the concentration of any of the TCEs in the aqueous phase (Fig. 3). The only exception was Ag-NPs with Ir (in the presence of Ag-NPs, 63μgL−1 Ir was detected in the solution, instead of the 50μgL−1 in the presence of soil only). Ag-NP is also the only NP which on its own and in the presence of HA increases Ge suspension stability by ~8μgL−1. Ge retention from soil is not increased under any other conditions. In solution A, Ce and Nd showed slightly higher aqueous concentrations (by ~5–10μgL−1) than the rest of the REEs for all the NPs tested. In solution B, Ir showed the highest concentrations: ~60% of all adsorbed concentration is recovered by all NPs in NP+HA experiments, Pd is the next most readily recovered, followed by Ru.

Most NPs are similarly effective in increasing TCE aqueous phase concentration, to a level of 10–20μgL−1 in the presence of HA, with Au NPs being relatively less efficient and CeO2 NPs being most efficient. The only notable behavior is exhibited by Ag-NPs (see above).

Note that in the ICP-MS measurement of solution A with NPs+HA, calibration standards which were measured repeatedly during all of the measurements as a reproducibility test yielded lower reproducibility compared to other ICP-MS runs: on average, error of 9%, with the highest for Pd and Ge (14%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of NPs and HA on aqueous concentrations of TCEs in sand solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 show results of a set of experiments that test addition of NPs, HA and NPs+HA to TCE solutions in the presence of sand, to establish their effect on the aqueous concentrations of TCEs like the one with soil described above (Section 3.3).

Elements in solution A (REEs) have two significant differences in their aqueous concentrations in sand solutions compared to soil solutions: (i) HA alone increases the aqueous concentrations of the REEs as efficiently as HA+NP; and (ii) REE retention on suspended NP+HA in sand solutions is about nine times greater than in soil solutions (Fig. 4).

HA on its own prevents REE retention by sand very efficiently (~90% remain in solution after 4h settling), while it does not increase REE suspension in soil solutions (Fig. 3). If the retention by sand and soil is sorption (or other types of bonding), a possible explanation is that HA is taking up sorption sites on sand grains and thus preventing REEs from sorption. In case of soil then, HA does not take up reaction sites, or at least not all of them, because soil has larger surface area than sand. The latter, however, is less likely as there was an excess (50mgL−1) of HA. Similarly, if the retention is precipitation, then HA interacts with those species of sand solution that in absence of HA interacts with REEs and precipitate. Alternatively, HA interacts directly with REEs, but in sand solutions this combination results in significantly higher aqueous concentration than in soil solutions.

As REE recovery with HA alone in sand solutions is similar to that of HA+NPs, the high REE recoveries in NP+HA experiments might be due to HA alone and do not suggest HA+NPs action as combination, as observed in soil solutions (see above).

Most individual NPs do not increase TCE concentration in the aqueous phase. Only Ag-NPs and Au-NPs sustain REEs in the aqueous phase without presence of HA. For the Ag-NPs sample, a recovery of ~90% was found, whereas for the Au ~7%. Among all the experiments, these are the only two NPs capable of increasing TCE aqueous concentration on their own. Al2O3-NPs, on the contrary, are the only NPs for which together with HA show lower aqueous concentrations of REEs than HA alone. In solution B, none of the conditions tested increased the aqueous concentration of Ir, which in soil solutions showed the highest increase in aqueous concentrations (it needs to be noted, however, that only ~15% of Ir is retained on sand). In contrast, in the presence of, individually, ZnO-NPs, Ag-NPs, and Ag-NPs+HA, less Ir remains in solution than in the presence of sand only, meaning that these NPs interact with Ir and settle out of solution. Pd and Ru aqueous concentrations show the highest increase by addition of NP+HA for all NPs. HA alone does not increase aqueous concentration of Pd, while, among NPs added on their own, Pd concentration in aqueous phase is slightly increased in the presence of Ag-NPs, Au-NPs and C-NPs (by ~20, 30 and 10μgL−1 respectively). Aqueous concentrations of Ru is increased by HA alone, as well as Ag-NPs and Au-NPs. Ge is not retained by sand solutions.

To identify the characteristics of NPs that might be particularly effective in stabilizing TCEs in the aqueous phase, we compared results of the batch experiments (Sections 3.1–3.4) to NP size and surface charge (zeta potential).

Despite the different sizes and surface charges upon addition to soil or sand solutions of the analyzed NPs, the REE (solution A) recovery from soil solutions is similar for all the NPs tested (Table 1 ): 1–2ppb with any of the NPs on their own and 14–27μgL−1 for NP+HA. Moreover, of the latter values, the minimum (14μgL−1) and the maximum (27μgL−1) recovery is achieved by Au and CeO2 NPs, respectively, which have very similar sizes (10–25nm and <25nm). In the initial 24h settling experiment (Fig. 2), CeO2 and Ag NPs were slightly more efficient in retaining NP+HA+REE in suspensions for longer time compared to the other tested NPs. In sand solutions, Ag-NPs and to smaller extent Au-NPs, significantly increase the aqueous concentration of REEs on their own (without presence of HA), but this phenomenon cannot be related to Ag/Au-NP size nor surface charge, compared to other NPs.

Similarly, none of the TCEs in solution B (Ir, Ge, Ru and Pd) exhibit aqueous concentrations that are related to NP size or surface charge: generally, each TCE shows similar values regardless of the NP type in both soil and sand solutions and both with and without HA. The only exception to this is Pd in sand solution, in the presence of NP only: in this case, aqueous Pd concentrations vary from 5 to 9ppb in the presence of ZnO and CeO2 (with very different average sizes: <100nm and <25nm, respectively) to 45ppb with Ag-NPs and Au-NPs (30–100nm and 10–30nm, respectively). These results suggest that a combination of a wide range of NPs, regardless of their size and charge characteristics and whether they are “natural” or engineered, might, in the presence of humic acid, potentially serve as “vehicles” for transport of retained TCEs. Furthermore, because the aqueous TCE concentrations in NP+HA experiments are similar for all of the tested NPs, the mechanism by which TCEs are kept in the aqueous phase might be the same for all the NPs and it is not NP specific. This phenomenon might be related to the nano-scale, because larger aluminum-based particles, for example, are used widely as a coagulant that causes aggregation of SPM in solutions; but in these batch experiments Al2O3 NPs show similar TCE concentrations compared to other tested NPs.

Results show that in experiments with soil, HA and NPs on their own do not increase aqueous concentrations of TCEs, while NP+HA increase TCE concentration (Section 3.3). In experiments with sand, however, HA increase TCEs concentrations as efficiently as HA+NP, while NPs on their own do not (Section 3.4). To better understand the effect of HA on TCE in solution, experiments with HA+NP without soil or sand, and in the presence of soil or sand were conducted with different HA concentrations: 10, 30 and 50mgL−1.

In experiments with no soil or sand, the concentration of solution A elements (REEs) in the aqueous phase was found to be proportional to HA concentration for all NPs except Ag-NPs and Au-NPs. In these experiments, ~40–80μgL−1 REEs were in solution in the presence of 10mgL−1 HA+NP (depending on NP), while ~100% REEs were in solution in presence of 30 and 50mgL−1 HA+NP, as well as 10mgL−1 HA+Ag-NPs or Au-NPs (Fig. 5 ). This suggests that all NPs, except Ag and Au, do interact with REEs in the presence of HA and the resulting particles settle if the concentration of HA present is not sufficient. This indicates that the HA concentration is critical for reducing the settling rate of REEs retained on NPs. This experiment thus shows interactions between all NPs+HA with REEs, except for Ag-NPs and Au-NPs. However, experiments on sand (Section 3.4) demonstrated that Ag-NPs and Au-NPs are the only NPs that increase REE concentrations on their own (without presence of HA). These two results suggest that Ag-NPs and Au-NPs directly interact with REEs and do not settle with time or relatively slowly (therefore, in presence of 10mgL−1 HA, only these two NPs do not yield lower REE concentration due to settling).

In samples with soil or sand, REE aqueous concentration is also proportional to HA concentration: increasing HA leads to higher REE concentrations in the aqueous phase, with two exceptions – for Al2O3-NPs and Ag-NPs in sand solutions.

In all experiments with HA, Y and La are present in solutions in slightly lower concentrations (by up to ~10μgL−1) than the rest of the REEs, suggesting that HA increases aqueous concentration of these two elements slightly less efficiently than others, or that Y and La are slightly less efficient in competition for suspension-favorable interactions than other REEs.

For solution B (right side of Fig. 5) the TCEs in the aqueous phase were found to be independent of HA concentration. Each element exhibited different behavior. In all HA+NPs experiments, regardless of NP type, except for Ag-NPs that yielded slightly lower concentration, Ir concentration in the solution is approximately the same (~80μgL−1) for all soil, sand and no sand/soil solutions. In the presence of soil only, aqueous Ir concentration was found to be ~50μgL−1 and in presence of sand, ~80μgL−1. Similarly for Ge: while none of the conditions tested decrease Ge retention by soil or sand, the same Ge concentration of ~90μgL−1 was measured in experiments with sand only and NPs+HA with no sand/soil. This might be a result of similar Ge and Ir retention rates during these experiments, without interaction with any of the NPs added in the experiments (note that the solution was buffered to pH7 prior the experiments, which might have changed TCE suspension stability).

In the presence of Ag-NPs, lower Ir concentrations were found in the solution for all experiments compared to other NPs, suggesting that Ag-NPs and Ir particles settle faster or have relatively smaller suspension stability.

Ru concentration in solution was ~90μgL−1 for all NP+HA combination with no sand/soil, ~70μgL−1 when sand was also added to the solution (compared to ~30μgL−1 in sand only retention experiment) and ~15μgL−1 when soil was part of the solution composition (compared to ~10μgL−1 in soil only retention experiment).

Pd exhibits unusual behavior: experiments with most NPs lead to increased Pd concentrations in solution when there is lower HA concentration. This is more evident in experiments with sand and for the cases where no sand or soil was added to the solution. For soil solutions, the effect is less pronounced with much lower concentrations in the solution. This might be due to “masking” of the pattern. Despite the possibly “negative” influence of HA concentration, Pd shows relatively high aqueous concentrations in the presence of HA+NPs in both soil and sand solutions (Figs. 3 and 4).

Overall, REE aqueous concentrations is generally dependent on HA concentration, suggesting direct interactions between HA and REEs, while HA concentration does not influence Ir, Ge and Ru and possibly has negative influence on Pd in solution B. With Ir and Ge, HA possibly does not interact at all (see above).

This study demonstrates that a combination of NPs and HA increases most TCE concentrations in the aqueous phase (here: concentration of TCEs in dissolved form and attached to SPM) of soil and sand solutions, regardless of the type of NP. TCEs are retained more strongly on soil than on sand, from which only combinations of NP+HA show substantial partitioning of TCEs to the aqueous phases, while neither HA, nor NPs on their own showed increased TCE concentrations in the aqueous phase. The enhancement of TCE aqueous concentrations due to the presence of NP+HA in sand solutions is about nine times stronger than in soil solutions. This increase occurs regardless of the type of NP, including “natural” and “anthropogenic” NPs. HA on its own increases TCE concentrations in sand solutions to a similar extent as HA+NP.

Among the NPs tested, the most notable ones are Ag-NPs, which demonstrate the highest potential for TCE transport, and Au-NPs: these are the only two NPs that were found to increase TCE stability in the aqueous phase in the absence of HA.

Among the TCEs, all REEs demonstrate similar behavior, with Ce and Nd showing slightly higher aqueous concentrations. For Tl and Ge however, none of the conditions tested induced partitioning to the aqueous phase.

This research was supported by a grant from the Ministry of National Infrastructure, Energy and Water Resources (Grant No. 215-17-016) in cooperation with the H2020 program “Water Joint Programming Initiative” through “WaterWorks2014” ERA-NET. The authors thank the Cathles group at Cornell University for providing samples of C-dots. B.B. holds the Sam Zuckerberg Professorial Chair in Hydrology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2017.08.170.
